method3.generated_article:
==Geography and ecology==
Jarvis Island is a small, uninhabited coral island located in the central equatorial Pacific Ocean at 0°22′S 160°01′W, approximately 40 km (25 mi) south of the equator.[2] With an area of about 4.5 km², it is one of the central Line Islands, also known as the Teraina Islands.[2] The island represents the crest of an ancient coral reef cap that lies atop a massive underlying extinct volcano, and its center consists of a dried former lagoon where deep guano deposits have accumulated.[2]

Jarvis has a tropical desert climate, characterized by high daytime temperatures, relatively steady easterly trade winds, and intense equatorial sun throughout the year.[2] The land is mostly sandy, reaching a maximum elevation of 7 m (23 ft) at its highest point.[2] The island has no known natural freshwater lens and receives scant rainfall of less than 1 m (40 inches) per year, supporting no plants larger than shrubs.[2] Its sparse vegetation of bunch grass, vines, and low-growing shrubs primarily serves as nesting, roosting, and sheltering habitat for seabirds, shorebirds, and numerous land hermit crabs.[2]

The marine ecosystem surrounding Jarvis is exceptionally productive due to the combined effects of wind-driven equatorial upwelling and topographic upwelling of the eastward-flowing Equatorial Undercurrent.[2] The shallow marine benthic habitats around Jarvis consist of a fringing reef with shallow back reefs, a steep forereef, and a moderate-sized reef terrace on the eastern windward side of the island.[2] The fringing reef is considered a healthy coral community, a condition attributed to the island's remoteness and lack of anthropogenic effects.[3]

Jarvis Island has more nesting seabirds than any other island in the U.S. tropical Pacific Islands.[2] It is home to an estimated one million sooty terns and supports 14 species of breeding seabirds, making it the only protected seabird island in the northern Line Islands.[1][3] The emergent land area of Jarvis encompasses 1,273 acres, while submerged lands and waters within the three-mile limit cover an additional 36,214 acres, for a total protected area of 37,487 acres.[3]

The island is managed as a National Wildlife Refuge with the stated purpose of preserving the complete ecosystem, both terrestrial and marine, with special emphasis given to the large seabird nesting colonies.[3] Jarvis is regarded as one of the last places in the world where terrestrial and marine tropical island ecosystems remain intact and relatively free of human impact, serving as a living laboratory for measuring current and future human impacts to island, coral reef, and deep marine habitats.[3] It has also been identified as an important site for the study of long-term global climate change and periodic phenomena such as the El Niño Southern Oscillation.[3]

The island's ecology was historically disrupted by the introduction of invasive species, including feral cats, which had significant effects on native seabird populations before their eradication.[4] Following the removal of invasive species, bird populations on Jarvis and nearby Howland and Baker Islands have shown recovery, including the return of both shearwater species to Jarvis.[1]

==History==
Jarvis Island was first sighted by Europeans on August 21, 1821, by Captain Brown aboard the British ship *Eliza Francis* (also spelled *Eliza Frances*), which was owned by Edward, Thomas, and William Jarvis, for whom the island was named.[1][2]

## Guano era and early claims

Following the discovery of guano deposits by American whalers and other seafarers exploring the central Pacific, the United States Exploring Expedition surveyed Jarvis in 1840.[2] In 1857, Alfred G. Benson and Charles H. Judd, aboard the Hawaiian schooner *Liholiho*, took formal possession of the island and claimed it for the American Guano Company of New York under the Guano Islands Act of 1856.[2][4] Later that same year, Commander Charles Henry Davis of the USS *St. Mary's* surveyed the island and formally claimed it for the United States, with the claim officially recorded on February 27, 1858.[1][4]

Guano was mined on Jarvis from 1857 until the island was abandoned in 1879, by which time approximately 8,000 tonnes of guano had been extracted.[1][5] The United Kingdom annexed the island in June 1889 but did not develop it further.[1][5] In 1906, Jarvis was leased to the Pacific Phosphate Company of London, but mining operations were again not pursued.[4]

## American reoccupation and wartime

The United States reclaimed the island in March 1935 as an unincorporated territory.[1] On May 13, 1936, President Franklin D. Roosevelt signed Executive Order 7368, placing control and jurisdiction of Jarvis Island with the Secretary of the Interior, under whose Office of Territorial Affairs it was initially administered.[3] In 1942, Japanese forces attacked the island during World War II, forcing the evacuation of the American colonists who had been stationed there.[1]

## National Wildlife Refuge

On June 27, 1974, the Secretary of the Interior designated Jarvis Island and its territorial sea extending to the 3-nautical-mile limit as a unit of the National Wildlife Refuge System (39 FR 27930).[3] The stated purpose of the refuge at the time of transfer to the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service was "the preservation of the complete ecosystem, terrestrial as well as marine," with special emphasis on the large seabird nesting colonies.[3] In 2009, Jarvis Island was further designated as part of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument.[5]

==Transportation==
Jarvis Island is accessible only by ocean-going marine vessels capable of traveling the open ocean for extended periods.[3] In recent years, transportation to the island has been provided by vessels operated by the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA), the U.S. Coast Guard, and private charter operators.[3]

The island is located approximately 1,263 nautical miles from the refuge management staff based in Honolulu, Hawaii, and on average it takes about 8 days to reach Jarvis by ship.[3] Typically, Jarvis is visited as part of a comprehensive biennial NOAA scientific ship expedition to the central Pacific Ocean insular properties, which also includes Baker Island, Howland Island, and Johnston Island National Wildlife Refuges.[3] The distance from refuge headquarters, combined with a lack of affordable and reliable transportation, a lack of infrastructure to support field operations, extreme environmental conditions, and safety concerns, poses significant operational limitations for the management of the island.[3]

Under some management alternatives considered by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, transportation to Jarvis and the other remote island refuges would be provided by a Service-owned vessel, enabling field camps to be rotated annually among the island refuges.[3] When fuel is transported to the island to support electrical power generation during expeditions, it must be carried in U.S. Department of Transportation approved containers that have been tested for leaks and weigh less than 50 pounds (23 kilograms) each.[1] No more than 200 gallons (757 liters) of fuel may be stored on Jarvis Island National Wildlife Refuge at any one time, and any unused fuel must be removed from the island after an expedition.[1]

==Military==
Although Jarvis Island has never hosted a permanent military garrison, its strategic location in the central equatorial Pacific made it relevant to United States defense planning in the mid-20th century. The American Equatorial Islands Colonization Project, initiated in 1935 by the U.S. Department of Commerce, placed young colonists—primarily Native Hawaiian men recruited from schools in Hawaii, including Kamehameha Schools—on Jarvis, Howland, and Baker Islands to establish weather stations and landing fields intended for both commercial aviation and military use on air routes between Australia and California.[1][2] A secondary objective was to reinforce U.S. sovereignty over the islands as a check against Japanese territorial expansion in the region.[1]

Physical traces of this era remain visible today. Short channels were blasted through the shallow fringing reefs in what are now designated the West and East Terrace georegions to allow small boats to land on the island in support of historical mining and military activities.[4] Residual debris and hazards from the guano mining and military periods also persist ashore and can occasionally wash up along the coastline.[3]

The military-related presence on Jarvis ended abruptly in early 1942, when the colonists—collectively known as the Hui Panalāʻau—were evacuated following Japanese attacks in the Pacific.[1] Their experiences were later documented in the 45-minute Bishop Museum film *Under a Jarvis Moon*, which chronicles the 1935–1942 colonization effort and the roles played by the more than 130 young men, 52 of them Hawaiian, who lived on Jarvis, Howland, and Baker Islands.[2] Since the evacuation, Jarvis Island has had no military use and is administered solely for conservation purposes under the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service.[3]

==National Wildlife Refuge==
Jarvis Island National Wildlife Refuge was established in 1974 and is located approximately 1,305 nautical miles south of Honolulu.[2] The refuge was created with the purpose of "the preservation of the complete ecosystem, terrestrial as well as marine," with special emphasis given to the large seabird nesting colonies on the island.[5] At the time of its establishment, the refuge boundary encompassed "all of said island … together with its territorial sea extending outward to the three-mile limit," covering 1,273 acres of emergent land and 36,214 acres of submerged lands and waters within the three-mile limit, for a total of 37,487 acres.[5]

On January 6, 2009, the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument was established, incorporating Jarvis Island National Wildlife Refuge within its boundaries.[2] Following this expansion, the refuge includes submerged lands out to 200 nautical miles from the island, encompassing a total of 429,853 acres, of which 1,273 acres are terrestrial and 428,580 acres are submerged.[2] The refuge is recorded in the World Database on Protected Areas as a Marine and Coastal Protected Area with a reported area of 1,744.87 km².[1]

The refuge is administered under the mission of the National Wildlife Refuge System, which seeks to manage a national network of lands and waters for the conservation, management, and, where appropriate, restoration of fish, wildlife, and plant resources and their habitats for the benefit of present and future generations of Americans.[2][5] Management goals for the refuge include conserving a diversity of fish, wildlife, and plants and their habitats—including endangered or threatened species—and maintaining a network of habitats for migratory birds, anadromous and interjurisdictional fish, and marine life.[5] A Comprehensive Conservation Plan for Jarvis Island National Wildlife Refuge was prepared by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service in 2008 to guide the long-term management of the refuge.[4]

Because of the refuge's remoteness, site visits by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service are infrequent; a visit by the Marine National Monuments of the Pacific Biology Team in August 2024, led by Beth Flint, Anna Vallery, and Meagan Selvig, was the first USFWS trip to Jarvis Island in over six years, during which the team conducted rapid assessments of the island's resources.[3] In 2024, the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service also issued a Draft Compatibility Determination evaluating refuge access for amateur radio operation, concluding that such activity would not detract from the refuge's core mission to protect and preserve the natural character of the fish, wildlife, plants, coral reef communities, and other resources associated with the tidal lands, submerged lands, and waters of the refuge.[2]
